Facts.text: Basiliscus becomes Byzantine Emperor, with a coronation ceremony in the Hebdomon palace in Constantinople
Last day for all Jews to leave Sicily
Dutch west coast hit by heavy storm, hundreds killed
Bayinnaung is crowned King of Burma, assembles the largest empire in the history of Southeast Asia
Pope Clement VIII seizes duchy of Ferrara on death of Alfonso
Brazilian city Belem (the entrance gate to the Amazon) founded by Captain Major Francisco Branco
Frisia & Groningen (Netherlands) begin use of Gregorian calendar
First public museum established in north American colonies in Charlestown, South Carolina
Franciscan Order establishes La Misió La Misión Santa Clara de Thamien in California
French evacuate Vienna
Gunpowder-ship explodes in Leiden, Netherlands, 150 die
The organizational meeting that led to the creation of the Wernerian Natural History Society, a former Scottish learned society, is held in Edinburgh.
British take Cayenne (French Guiana) from French (until 1814)
1st cargo arrives in New Orleans by steam, from Natchez
France decrees Bonaparte family excluded from the country forever
Astronomical Society of London (now the Royal Astronomical Society) founded in England
Battle of Wetumka, Fla
Anthracite coal 1st used to smelt iron, Mauch Chunk, Pennsylvania
Franciscan nuns begin missionary work on Netherland Antilles
Unsuccessful People's Uprising against the ruling Bourbons begins in Palermo, Sicily, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies
Florida state troops demand surrender of Fort Pickens (US Civil War)
Confederate President Davis delivers his "State of Confederacy" address
The Royal Aeronautical Society is formed in London
Aleksey Konstantinovich Tolstoy's play "Smert Ioanna Groznogo" (The Death of Ivan the Terrible) premieres in St Petersburg
Yohannes IV is crowned Emperor of Ethiopia in Axum, 1st imperial coronation in Axum in over 200 years
Kwang-su becomes Emperor of China
The National Trust is founded in Britain
1st X-ray photo in US (Dr Henry Smith, Davidson NC)
Lynmouth Lifeboat rescues 18 people from the stricken schooner Forest Hall off the coast of Devon
Utopian Freeland Colony founded on Whidbey Island, in Washington State, US
Uddevalla Suffrage Association is formally dissolved
Herero people of South West Africa, now Namibia, rebel against German colonial rule
1st time Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 100 (100.26)
Football rules committee legalizes forward pass
Britain grants responsible government to former colony of Transvaal
A long-distance radio message is sent from the Eiffel Tower for the first time.
The University of the Philippines College of Law is formally established; three future Philippine presidents are among the first enrollees.
-47°F (-44°C), Washta, Iowa (state record)
US House of Representatives rejects proposal to give women right to vote
Britain proclaims Gilbert & Ellice Islands as a colony in Pacific
Max Immelmann and Oswald Boelcke receive the Pour le Merite, the German Empire's highest military award, for achieving eight aerial victories each over Allied aircraft
Finland's "Mosaic Confessors" law comes into effect, making Finnish Jews full citizens
Annual drafting of baseball players from minor leagues to be done in inverse order of the final standings, agreed to
History of Science Society organized at Boston
John Howard Lawson's "Processional" premieres in NYC
Seatrain (RR cars on ships) service begins, New Orleans-Havana
Hattie W Caraway elected 1st woman senator (D-Ark)
Philip Barry's play "The Animal Kingdom" premieres in NYC
Uprising of Guardia Civil in Spain, 25 die
US Congress recognizes the Philippines' independence
Plough for laying submarine cable patented
World War II: Soviets bombs cities in Finland.
British troops reconquer Sollum, Egypt
Dutch troops on Tarakan island off North Borneo surrender the island's oil fields to the Japanese
US National War Labor Board created to mediate labor disputes
Frankfurters replaced by Victory Sausages (mix of meat & soy meal)
Failed resistance raid on distribution office of Borgerstraat, Amsterdam
German forces in Belgium retreat in Battle of Bulge
The Soviets begin a large offensive against the Nazis in Eastern Europe
US Task Force 38 destroys 41 Japanese ships in Battle of South China Sea
Edouardo de Filippo's  stage drama "Questi Fantasmi!" premieres in Rome
NFL champs Cleveland Rams owner Daniel Reeves announces plans to move team to Los Angeles, to avoid having to compete with the incoming AAFC Browns team
First Supermarket in the UK opens - the Co-op, the country’s first permanent self-service store, in East London’s Manor Park
US Supreme Court decision  in Sipuel v Oklahoma State Board of Regents
Dutch court affirms death sentence against SS chief Hanns Rauter
Swedish tanker rams British submarine Truculent in Thames, 64 die
USSR re-introduces death penalty for treason, espionage & sabotage
University of Tennessee admits its 1st black student, Gene Mitchell Gray, as a graduate student in chemistry
9 "Jewish" physicians arrested for "terrorist activities" in Moscow
Austria's worst avalanche-kills 200; 9hrs later 2nd one-kills 115
FBi arrests 6 members of the Great Brink's robbery gang, 6 days before statute of limitations runs out
NCAA adds 2 point conversion to football scoring
KOED TV channel 11 in Tulsa, Oklahoma (PBS) begins broadcasting
Rediscovery of the Caves of Nerja in Malaga, Spain
Syracuse NationalDolph Schayesis the 1st NBA player to score 15,000 points
UN genocide pact goes into effect
Operation Chopper begins, America's first combat mission in the Vietnam War
"Go Away Little Girl" by Steve Lawrence peaks at #1
Spin bowler Bobby Simpson takes 5-57 for Australia v England
Revolution overthrows Sultan of Zanzibar, 1 month after independence
"Hullabaloo" premieres on NBC-TV
At 10:58 am PST burn up a nuclear rocket in Nevada
12 day NYC transit strike ends
3 British Members of Parliament are attacked in a hotel in Salisbury, Rhodesia (now Harare, Zimbabwe)
Dr. James Bedford becomes the first person to be cryonically preserved with intent of future resuscitation; remains preserved at the Alcor Life Extension Foundation
NBC revival of Jack Webb's police drama "Dragnet", now broadcast in color, debuts; runs through 1970
Beatles Film Production Ltd changes name to Apple Film Production Ltd
Nighttime version of "Hollywood Squares" premieres on NBC TV
Atlantic Records releases eponymous debut album of Led Zeppelin in US
"All in the Family" premieres on CBS featuring 1st toilet flush on TV
Congressional Black Caucus organizes
Negotiations over price of petroleum begin in Tehran between 6 OPEC Persian Gulf states and 22 oil companies
Two bombs explode at UK Employment Secretary Robert Carr's home
Tigers sign a lease to build a $126M domed stadium (doesn't happen)
Libya & Tunisia announces they are merging as "Islamic Arab Republic"
Chrysler Corp offers 1st car rebates
Super Bowl IX, Tulane Stadium, New Orleans, LA: Pittsburgh Steelers beat Minnesota Vikings, 16-6; MVP: Franco Harris, Pittsburgh, RB
UN Security Council votes 11-1 to seat Palestine Liberation Organization
Anti-French demonstrations takes place in Israel after Paris released Abu Daoud, responsible 1972 Munich massacre of Israeli athletes
LA's Hillside Strangler, Kenneth Bianchi, arrested in Bellingham
Record blizzard strikes the US midwest killing over 100
Phoenix point guard Mike Bratz begins NBA free throw streak of 57 games during a 107-96 loss at the Golden State Warriors
-35°F (-37°C), Chester, Massachusetts (state record)
MLB Cincinnati Reds become last team to sign a free agent, outfielder Larry Biitner
NCAA creates football Kickoff Classic to begin in August
Quebec Nordiques play 230th NHL game without being shut out
24th space shuttle (61-C) mission-Columbia 7-launched
6 claim to survive in rubble, 35 days after Armenian quake (hoax)
Romania bans Communist party (1st Warsaw Pact member to do so)
Largest crowd to watch Atlantic Coast Women's Basketball game (11,520)
Melvin Stewart swims world record 200 m butterfly (1:55.69)
A new constitution, providing for freedom to form political parties, is approved by a referendum in Mali.
Algeria's general elections canceled after strong gains by Islamic Salvation Front in the 1st round
Highest scoring NCAA basketball game; Troy State Trojans defeat DeVry Hoyas 258-149 in Troy, Alabama
Last building in Gateway area in Cleveland is demolished
Major earthquake kills 5,092 in Kobe, Japan
PopeJohn Paul IIarrives on his second visit to the Philippines on trip to South East Asia
Russian troops arrive in Bosnia as part of a joint operation with the US
Ladies' figure skating star Oksana Baiul injured slightly while driving intoxicated in Bloomfield, Connecticut
Space Shuttle STS 81 (Atlantis 18), launches into space
Nineteen European nations agree to forbid human cloning.
Downtown Disney opens to the public in Anaheim
The world's largest ocean liner, RMS Queen Mary 2, makes its maiden voyage
Deep Impact (space mission) launches from Cape Canaveral on a Delta 2 rocket.
A stampede during the Stoning the Devil ritual on the last day at the Hajj in Mina, Saudi Arabia, kills at least 362 Muslim pilgrims
French warship Clemenceau reaches Egypt and is barred access to the Suez Canal. Greenpeace activists board the ship.

The foreign ministers of the United Kingdom, France, and Germany declare that negotiations with Iran over its nuclear program have reached a dead end and recommend that Iran be referred to the United Nations Security Council.
Comet McNaught reaches perihelion becoming the brightest comet in more than 40 years.
Colombia officially leaves the recession after achieving 2% economic growth in the last quarter of 2009

"Mile High Miracle", In AFC Divisional Playoff Game Denver Broncos ahead of Baltimore Ravens 35-28 with 1:09 remaining, quarterback Joe Flacco heaves 70 yard touchdown pass to receiver Jacoby Jones to tie the game. Ravens win in overtime.
A failed attempt to rescue a French hostage in Bulo Marer, Somalia, results in 18 deaths
Beijing’s level of air pollution is declared to be at levels that are hazardous to human health
Stephen Bunting and Lisa Ashton win the 2014 BDO World Darts Championship
FIFA Ballon d'Or: Real Madrid & Portugal forwardCristiano Ronaldowins for a 2nd consecutive time; his 3rd award overall; VfL Wolfsburg midfielder Nadine Keßler is women's award winner
18-year-old Rahaf Mohammed al-Qunun arrives in Toronto, Canada, as a refugee, via Thailand, after escaping family abuse in Saudi Arabia

1st ATP Cup Men's Tennis, Sydney:Novak Đoković& Viktor Troicki beat Spanish pair Pablo Carreño Busta & Feliciano López 6-3, 6-4 to clinch inaugural title for Serbia, 2-1; Đoković beats Rafael Nadal 6-2, 7-6

Argentine striker Sergio Agüero becomes top-scoring overseas player in English Premier League football history with a hat-trick in Manchester City's 6-1 rout of Aston Villa; total 177 takes him past Thierry Henry
Taal volcano, 70km (45 miles) south of Manila in the Philippines begins erupting prompting evacuations
India's Supreme Court puts on hold three controversial new farm laws that ignited weeks of protests by farmers in Delhi
Toadzilla, a large cane toad and unofficially the world's largest toad at 2.7kg, discovered in Conway National Park, Australia (as an invasive pest later euthanized)
The island nation of Cape Verde is the first sub-Saharan country to be declared malaria-free in 50 years by the WHO
“Wokery”, “safe word”, “forever chemical” and “swear box” added to the latest update of the Oxford English Dictionary
Gustav Iof Sweden is crowned King of Sweden, rules for 37 years, and becomes known as the "father of the nation"
British KingCharles IIgrants a charter for The Royal Society after its establishment in 1660, officially renamed The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge, af
George Frideric Handel's opera "Ottone" premieres at the King's Theatre London
US Senate approvesThomas Jefferson's nomination ofJames Monroeand Robert Livingstone to negotiate purchase of New Orleans from France
HMS Beagle withCharles Darwinreaches Sydney, Australia
Itō Hirobumibegins his third term as Prime Minister of Japan
Harry Houdiniperforms at Rembrandt theater, Amsterdam
Henry Campbell-Bannerman's cabinet (which includedH. H. Asquith,David Lloyd George, and Winston Churchill) embarks on sweeping social reforms after a Liberal landslide in the British general election
After using other pseudonyms over the years, Josef Dzhugashvili signs himself asStalin("man of steel") in a letter to the newspaper Social Democrat
Montreal Canadien's centerJoe Malonescores 5 goals in a 9-4 win over Ottawa to become the first 20 goal scorer in NHL history; goes on to score 44 regular season goals
Philip Barry andElmer Rice's play "Cock Robin" premieres in NYC
Timely Comics (laterMarvel) founded by American publisher Martin Goodman in New York
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchilland French GeneralCharles de Gaullebegin a two-day wartime conference in Marrakesh
Mahatma Gandhibegins his final fast
"Arthur Godfreyand his Friends" premieres on CBS TV
Ezzard CharlesTKOs Lee Oma in 10 for heavyweight boxing title
Queen Elizabeth IIopens New Zealand parliament
Southern Christian Leadership Council (SCLC) founded withMartin Luther King Jr.as leader at Ebenezer Church in Atlanta
Syracuse NationalDolph Schayessets NBA record at 11,770 points
Berry GordyJr.'s Tamla Records (later Motown) begins operating in Detroit, Michigan
"Batman", starring Adam West as Batman, Burt Ward as Robin, andCesar Romeroas The Joker, debuts on ABC
Boston Celtics coachRed Auerbachrecords his 1,000th NBA career victory (regular season and playoffs), 114-102 over the Los Angeles Lakers in Boston
LBJsays US should stay in South Vietnam until communist aggression ends
Louisville, Kentucky draft board refuses exemption for boxerMuhammad Ali
Super Bowl III, Orange Bowl, Miami, FL: New York Jets beat Baltimore Colts, 16-7; MVP:Joe Namath, NY Jets, QB
US Federal grand jury indicts Rev Philip Berrigan & 5 others, including a nun & 2 priests, on charges of plotting to kidnapHenry Kissinger
Abu Sayeed Chudhury becomes President of Bangladesh andSheikh Mujibur RahmanPrime Minister
"Joker" bySteve MillerBand peaks at #1
Soap opera "Dynasty"produced by Aaron Spelling and starringJohn Forsythe,Linda EvansandJoan Collinspremieres on ABC-TV
Brooks RobinsonandJuan Marichalelected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Britain'sPrince Edwardresigns from his Royal Marines training
Ex-dictator of UgandaIdi Aminexpelled from Zaire
Civil rights activist ReverendAl Sharptonis stabbed in Bensonhurst, Brooklyn
US Congress givesGeorge H. W. Bushauthority to wage war against Iraq
NHL greatMario Lemieuxannounces that he has been diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma
Malcolm X's daughter arrested for plotting Louis Farrakham's murder
10th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: The Allman Brothers Band;Al Green;Janis Joplin; Led Zeppelin; Martha and the Vandellas;Neil Young;Frank Zappa; The Orioles; Paul Ackerman
Murder trial againstO.J. Simpson, begins in LA
13th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: The Eagles;Fleetwood Mac; TheMamas & the Papas; Lloyd Price; Santana; Gene Vincent; "Jelly Roll" Morton; andAllen Toussaint
Disney Channel TV series "Lizzie McGuire" premieres starringHilary Duff
During an emotional ceremony,Mark Messier's #11 jersey is retired by the New York Rangers
Turkey releasesMehmet Ali Ağcafrom jail after he served 25 years for shooting PopeJohn Paul II
Cristiano Ronaldois awarded 2008 FIFA World Player of the Year for the first time at a ceremony in Zurich
"True Detective", a crime-drama starringWoody HarrelsonandMatthew McConaughey, debuts on HBO
Michael Bay's film "13 Hours", based on the 2012 Benghazi attack, is released
Justin Thomas(23) becomes the youngest player to shoot a sub-60 round of 59 in the opening round of the Sony Open at Waialae CC in Hawaii; he also wins the tournament
US PresidentBarack Obamain surprise presentation awards Vice PresidentJoe Bidenwith the Presidential Medal of Freedom
Missy Elliottis the first female rapper to be inducted into the Songwriter's Hall of Fame
UK PMBoris Johnsonadmits he attended a "bring your own booze" staff party in May 2020 during the country's first lockdown
Brock Purdyis named the NFC Rookie of the Month for December and January
US Attorney GeneralMerrick Garlandappoints a special counsel to investigate PresidentJoe Biden's handling of classified documents

GovernorGavin Newsomsays the California wildfires will be one of the worst natural disasters in US history; having blazed through  40,000 acres, damaged about 12,000 structures and killing at least 24 people
Holland begins use of Gregorian calendar (yesterday was 1/1/1583)
Operation Ranch Hand begins, a US Air Force operation to spray South Vietnamese forests with defoliants such as Agent Orange
Earthquake devastates Haiti, killing approximately 160,000 and destroying the majority of the capital Port-au-Prince
Henry III of Nassau-Breda, Dutch Stadholder of Holland and Zeeland (1515-1521), born in Siegen, Nassau (d. 1538)
Andreas Alicatus, Italian jurist and humanist, born in Alzate Brianza, Duchy of Milan (d. 1550)
Charles Emanuel I, the Great, Italian Duke of Savoy (Peace of Lyon), born in Rivoli, Savoy (d. 1630)
Peter Scriverius, Dutch lawyer and historian, born in Haarlem (d. 1660)
Jan Baptist van Helmont, Flemish chemist (found boiling point temperature), born in Brussels (d. 1644)
Alexander Ruthven, Scottish nobleman who attempted to kidnap King James, born in Perth, Scotland (d. 1600)
Jusepe de Ribera, Spanish painter and printmaker nicknamed "Lo Spagnoletto / the Little Spaniard", born in Xàtiva, Spain (d. 1652)
François Duquesnoy, French sculptor (statue of Saint Andrew in the transept of St Peter's), born in Brussels (d. 1643)
Adrian van Utrecht, Flemish still life painter, born in Antwerp (d. 1652)
Ernst Rüdiger von Starhemberg, Austrian field marshal (1683 successfully defended Vienna against Ottoman siege), born in Graz, Styria (d. 1701)
Gaetano Latilla, Italian opera composer, born in Bari, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1788)
Jacques Duphly, French composer and harpsichordist, born in Rouen, France (d. 1789)
Antonio de Ulloa, Spanish scientist, general and the first Spanish Governor of Louisiana, born in Seville, Spain (d. 1795)
Duke Ferdinand of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel, German general during the Seven Years' War, born in Wolfenbüttel, Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg (d. 1792)
Samuel Langdon, American college educator, President of Harvard (1774-80), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1797)
Lazzaro Spallanzani, Italian physiologist and biologist, born in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (now Italy) (d. 1799)
Johann Joachim Christoph Bode, German bassoonist and composer, born in Barum, Electorate of Brunswick-Lüneburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1793)
Brizio Petrucci, Italian composer, born in Massalom-barda, Ravenna, Papal States (d. 1838)
Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi, Swiss educator (Leonard & Gertrude), born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1827)
Ferdinand Trauttmansdorff, Austrian diplomat and statesman (ruled the Netherlands for Holy Roman Emperor), born in Vienna (d. 1827)
Ferdinand I, King of the Two Sicilies (1816-1825) and Naples, born in Naples (d. 1825)
Jacob Michael Reinhold Lenz, Baltic German poet and dramatist (The Soldiers), born in Sesswegen, Governorate of Livonia, Russian Empire (d. 1792)
Mikhail Speransky, Russian reformer and advisor to Alexander I, born in Cherkutino, Russia (d. 1839)
Wilhelm Martin Leberecht de Wette, German exegetist and theologist, born in Ulla, Thuringia (d. 1849)
Erik Gustaf Geijer, Swedish historian and poet (Natthimmelen), born in Geijersgården, Sweden (d. 1847)
Robert Inglis, English Conservative politician known for his staunch high church views, born in Snelston, England (d. 1855)
Johan August Arfwedson, Swedish chemist who discovered the element lithium, born in Skaraborg County, Seden (d. 1841)[1]
Robert Patterson, Irish-American Major General (Union Army), born in Cappagh, County Tyrone, Ireland (d. 1881)
Gideon Brecher, Austrian physician and Jewish writer, born in Prossnitz, Moravia (d. 1873)
Louise Sophie Blussé, Dutch author, born in Leiden, Holland (d. 1896)
Hippolyte Monpou, French organist and opera composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1841)
Jan Theodoor Beelen, Dutch-Belgian bible scholar, born in Amsterdam (d. 1884)
Paul "the Great" Taglioni, Italian-Austrian ballet choreographer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1884)
Ferdinand II, King of the Two Sicilies (1830-59), born in Palermo (d. 1859)
Jones Mitchell Withers, American soldier and Confederate Major General, born in Huntsville, Alabama (d. 1890)
Zealous Bates Tower, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Cohasset, Massachusetts (d. 1900)
Nikolai Afanisev, Russian composer and violinist, born in Tobolsk, Russia (d. 1898)
Étienne Lenoir, Belgian-French engineer and inventor, built the first commercially combustion engine, born in Mussy-la-Ville, Belgium (d. 1900)
Joseph Robert Davis, American aide-de-camp and nephew to Confederate President Jefferson Davis, born in Louisiana (d. 1896)
Charles Cruft, American teacher, lawyer, railroad executive, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Terre Haute, Indiana (d. 1883)
Richard Waterhouse, American soldier and Confederate general, born in Rhea County, Tennessee, (d. 1876)
Adolf Jensen, German pianist, composer, and teacher, born in Königsberg, Prussia (d. 1879)
Carlos Troyer, American composer, born in Frankfurt, German Confederation (d. 1920)
Francois Coppee, French poet, born in Paris (d. 1908)
Jean Béraud, French Belle Époque painter, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1935)
Joseph Joffre, French army Chief of Staff (Indo China, WWI Battle of Marne), born in Rivesaltes, France (d. 1931)
Gregorio Ricci-Curbastro, Italian mathematician (tensor analysis), born in Lugo di Romagna, Italy (d. 1925)
John Singer Sargent, American painter (Wyndham Sisters), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1925)
James Mark Baldwin, American philosopher and psychologist, born in Columbia, South Carolina (d. 1934)
Swami Vivekananda, Indian Hindu spiritual leader and a key figure in the introduction of Yoga to the Western world, born in Kolkata, India (d. 1902)
Karel Burian (also Carl Burrian), Czech operatic tenor, born in Rousínov, Moravia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1924)
Paul Guthnick, German astronomer, born in Hitdorf am Rhein (d. 1947)
Spyridon Louis, Greek athlete who won the 1st modern Olympics marathon in 1896, born in Marousi, Greece (d. 1940)
Marika Stiernstedt, Swedish author (Ulla Bella), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1954)
Frank J. Corr, American lawyer and politician (acting Mayor of Chicago for 6 weeks in 1933), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1934)
Ferenc Molnar, Hungarian American playwright (A Pal Utrai Fiuk) and novelist (The Paul Street Boys), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1952)
Ray Harroun, American auto racer (inaugural Indianapolis 500 1911; Motorsports Hall of Fame of America 2000), born in Spartansburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1968)
Jakob Jud, Swiss linguist and etymologist, born in Wängi, Switzerland (d. 1952)
Milton Sills, American actor (The Sea Hawk), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1930)
(Mary Louise) "Texas" Guinan, American vaudeville singer, silent film actress, and speakeasy (saloon) proprietor, born in Waco, Texas (d. 1933)
Louis Horst, American dance choreographer and composer, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1964)
Claude Delvincourt, French composer and pianist, born in Paris, France (d. 1954)
Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, American pastor and educator, born in Paris, Tennessee (d. 1976)
Mikhail Gurevich, Russian aircraft designer, co-founded  Mikoyan-Gurevich military aviation bureau, born in Rubanshchina, Kursk, Russia (d. 1976)
Alfred Rosenberg, German Nazi official who wrote Nazi idealogue "The Myth of the Twentieth Century", born in Reval, Estonia, Russian Empire (d. 1946)
Georges Carpentier, French boxer (world light heavyweight champion; 1920-22; 1st $1m gate v J Dempsey), born in Lievin, France (d. 1975)
David Wechsler, American psychologist who developed intelligence scales, born in Lespezi, Romania, (d. 1981)
Hendrik Valk, Dutch painter, cartoonist and graphic artist, born in Zoeterwoude, Netherlands (d. 1986)
Jose Forns y Cuadras, Spanish composer and teacher, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1952)
Paul H Muller, Swiss chemist (DDT-Nobel 1948), born in Olten, Switzerland (d. 1965)
Pierre Bernac, French baritone, born in Paris, France (d. 1979)
Väinö Hannikainen, Finnish harpist, and composer, born in Jyväskylä, Grand Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 1960)
Ray Teal, American actor (Judgment at Nuremberg, Bonanza), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1976)
Simon Spoor, Dutch army intelligence officer (WWII; Indonesian National Revolution), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1949)
Igor Kurchatov, Soviet-Russian nuclear physicist who directed the Soviet atomic bomb project, born in Sim, Chelyabinsk Oblast, Russia (d. 1960)
"Mississippi" Fred McDowell, American blues singer and guitarist, born in Rossville, Tennessee (d. 1972)
James Bennett Griffin, American archaeologist, born in Atchison, Kansas (d. 1997)
Marie Paxton, British stage and screen actress (Ouanga; Kind Lady), born in London, England (d. 1992)
(Woodward) "Tex" Ritter, American country singer (5 Star Jubilee, "Wayward Wind"), born in Murvaul, Texas (d. 1974)
Clement Hurd, American illustrator, born in New York City (d. 1988)
José Limón, Mexican-American dancer and choreographer (The Moor's Pavane; I, Odysseus), born in Culiacán, Mexico (d. 1972)
Mauritz Hugo, Swedish actor (Atomic Blonde, Death Valley Gunfighter), born in Ostergotland, Sweden (d. 1974)
Patsy Kelly, American actress (Rosemary's Baby, North Ave Irregulars), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1981)
Tony Morabito, American football executive who founded and co-owned the San Francisco 49ers, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1957)
Charles Moeller, Belgian theologist (Humanism et saintete), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1986)
Ivan Getting American physicist and engineer (developed Global Positioning System), born in New York City (d. 2003)[1]
Edward Gurney, American attorney and politician (US Senator for Florida, 1969-74; US Representative for Florida, 1963-69), born in Portland, Maine (d. 1996)
Ernest Armstrong, British politician (L) & teacher, born in Stanley, County Durham (d. 1996)
Frank Annunzio, American politician (Rep-D-IL, 1965-93), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2001)
Joseph-Aurèle Plourde, Catholic archbishop of Ottawa, born in Saint-François-de-Madawaska, Canada (d. 2013)
Martin Agronsky, American journalist (1952 Peabody, 1961 DuPont-Columbia), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1999)
Paul Jarrico, American writer, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1997)
Roy Ralston, British pilot (WWII bomber pilot), born in Moss Side, Manchester (d. 1996)
House Peters Jr., American actor (King of the Rocket Man), born in New Rochelle, New York (d. 2008)
Jay McShann, American jazz pianist and bandleader, born in Muskogee, Oklahoma (d. 2006)
William Pleeth, British cellist and teacher of Jacqueline du Pré, born in London, England (d. 1999)
Jimmy Skinner, Canadian hockey coach, born in Selkirk, Canada (d. 2007)
Walter Hendl, American conductor and composer, born in West New York, New Jersey (d. 2007)
James Farmer, American civil rights leader, born in Marshall, Texas (d. 1999)
Mac Speedie, American Pro Football HOF end (AAFC C'ship 1946-1949; NFL C'ship 1950; 6 × First-Team All-Pro; Pro Bowl 1950, 52; Cleveland Browns), born in Odell, Illinois (d. 1993)
Prof Jerzy Zubrzycki, Polish-born Australian sociologist, born in Kraków, Poland (d. 2009)
John Davis, American weightlifter (Olympic gold bantamweight 1948, heavyweight 1952), born in Smithtown, New York (d. 1984)
Leo Smit, American classical pianist, composer (The Alchemy of Love), teacher and photographer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1999)
Tadeusz Żychiewicz, Polish journalist and art historian, born in Bratkowice, Poland (d. 1994)
Ira Hayes, Pima Native American soldier, born in Sacaton, Arizona (d. 1955)
Jan Willem Radecker, Dutch sculptor and son of John Raedecker, born in Schoorl, Netherlands (d. 2009)
Olivier Gendebien, Belgian auto racer (24 hours of Le Mans 1958, 60-62), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1998)
Bill Burrud, American television host (Safari to Adventure, Animal World), born in Hollywood, California (d. 1990)
Katherine "Scottie" MacGregor, American actress (Little House On The Prairie - "Harriet Oleson"), born in Glendale, California (d. 2018)
Laurentiu Profeta, Romanian composer, born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 2006)
Ralph Legall, West Indian cricket wicket-keeper (4 Tests), born in St Michael, Barbados (d. 2003)
Morton Feldman, American composer (For Philip Guston; Rothko Chapel), born in New York City (d. 1987)
Ray Price, American country music singer-songwriter, and guitarist ("For the Good Times"; "You're the Best Thing That Ever Happened To Me"), born in Perryville, Texas (d. 2013)
Salvatore Martirano, American composer, born in Yonkers, New York (d. 1995)
Lew Gallo, American character actor and screenwriter (Get Smart; Gunsmoke; 12 O'Clock High), born in Mount Kisco, New York (d. 2000)
Ruth Brown, American actress and singer sometimes known as the "Queen of R&B" ("So Long"; "Teardrops From My Eyes"), born in Portsmouth, Virginia (d. 2006)
Vic Toweel, South African boxer (undisputed world bantamweight champion), born in Benoni, South Africa (d. 2008)
Alasdair MacIntyre, Scottish-American philosopher (After Virtue), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2025)[1]

Glenn Yarborough, American pop-folk singer and guitarist (The Limeliters, 1959-63 - "A Dollar Down", "Red Roses and White Wine"; solo "Baby the Rain Must Fall", "Jubilee"), sailor, and philanthropist, born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 2016)
Jennifer Johnston, Irish author (Fool's Sanctuary), born in Dublin, Ireland
